explanation: Protocol Buffers (protobuf) is a binary serialization format — smaller and faster than JSON. You define message structures in `.proto` files, and the protoc compiler generates code for any language. Protobuf uses field numbers (not names) for encoding, making it compact and backward-compatible.
key_takeaway: Protobuf defines service contracts in `.proto` files. Field numbers ensure backward compatibility — you can add fields without breaking existing clients.
try_it: Define a proto file for an order service with CreateOrder, GetOrder, and ListOrders RPCs. Generate Python stubs and verify the generated code.

explanation: gRPC supports four communication patterns: unary (request-response like REST), server streaming (server sends multiple responses), client streaming (client sends multiple requests), and bidirectional streaming (both sides stream). Streaming is ideal for real-time data, large uploads, or long-running operations.
key_takeaway: Unary for simple RPCs, server streaming for real-time feeds, client streaming for uploads, bidirectional streaming for chat/real-time collaboration.
try_it: Implement a bidirectional streaming chat service where clients send messages and the server echoes them back with timestamps.

explanation: gRPC uses status codes (OK, NOT_FOUND, PERMISSION_DENIED, etc.) for error handling — similar to HTTP status codes but more granular. Deadlines specify how long a client is willing to wait — if the deadline passes, the server can stop processing and the client gets a DEADLINE_EXCEEDED error.
key_takeaway: Use gRPC status codes for error classification. Always set deadlines on client calls to prevent hanging. Server should check `context.is_active()` for long-running operations.
try_it: Implement a service that returns DEADLINE_EXCEEDED for slow operations. Set a 1-second deadline on the client and verify the error is caught.

explanation: gRPC uses HTTP/2, which maintains persistent connections — traditional HTTP load balancers that route per-request don't work well. Client-side load balancing lets the client pick which server to call. Proxy-based load balancing (like Envoy or gRPC-LB) handles it transparently.
example:
```python
import grpc

# Client-side load balancing
channel = grpc.insecure_channel(
    'localhost:50051',
    options=[
        ('grpc.lb_policy_name', 'round_robin'),
        ('grpc.enable_retries', 1),
        ('grpc.service_config', '{
            "loadBalancingConfig": [{"round_robin": {}}],
            "methodConfig": [{
                "name": [{"service": "users.UserService"}],
                "retryPolicy": {
                    "maxAttempts": 3,
                    "initialBackoff": "0.1s",
                    "maxBackoff": "1s",
                    "backoffMultiplier": 2,
                    "retryableStatusCodes": ["UNAVAILABLE"]
                }
            }]
        }')
    ]
)

# Health checking
from grpc_health.v1 import health_pb2, health_pb2_grpc

health_stub = health_pb2_grpc.HealthStub(channel)
response = health_stub.Check(health_pb2.HealthCheckRequest(service="users.UserService"))
print(response.status)  # SERVING, NOT_SERVING, UNKNOWN
```
key_takeaway: gRPC needs client-side or proxy-based load balancing (not per-request). Use round-robin with health checking. Envoy is the standard proxy for gRPC traffic.
try_it: Set up two gRPC servers on different ports with client-side round-robin load balancing. Verify requests are distributed evenly.

explanation: Browsers can't make gRPC calls directly because gRPC relies on HTTP/2 features (trailing headers, binary framing) that browsers don't expose to JavaScript. gRPC-Web is a protocol that bridges this gap — it uses a proxy (Envoy) that translates between gRPC-Web (browser) and standard gRPC (server).
key_takeaway: gRPC-Web bridges browsers and gRPC via an Envoy proxy. Use the same `.proto` files for server and browser clients. Envoy handles protocol translation transparently.
try_it: Set up a gRPC server, configure Envoy as a gRPC-Web proxy, and make calls from a browser using generated JavaScript stubs.

explanation:
gRPC interview problems focus on Protocol Buffer schema design, streaming patterns, and error handling. The key patterns are: defining .proto files with proper message and service definitions, implementing unary and streaming RPCs, handling deadlines and cancellation, and intercepting requests for auth/logging.

Unlike REST's JSON-over-HTTP, gRPC uses binary Protocol Buffers over HTTP/2, making it 2-10x faster for serialization. Streaming RPCs enable real-time bidirectional communication. Practice designing proto schemas that are backward-compatible using field numbers and optional fields.
key_takeaway: gRPC uses Protocol Buffers for type-safe, binary-serialized RPC. Streaming RPCs enable real-time communication. Always use field numbers for backward compatibility.
try_it: Define a .proto file for a bookstore service with unary RPCs for CRUD and a server-streaming RPC for browsing books.

practice_challenges.title: Define a CRUD Service in Proto
practice_challenges.hint: Define messages for Request/Response and a Service with four RPCs: Create, Read, Update, Delete.
practice_challenges.description: Design a .proto file for a TaskManager service with Create, Get, Update, and Delete RPCs for tasks.
practice_challenges.example_input: Requirements: tasks with title, description, status, and due date
practice_challenges.example_output: Complete .proto file with TaskManager service and all message types

practice_challenges.title: Implement Server Streaming RPC
practice_challenges.hint: Use 'stream' keyword in the return type. The server yields multiple responses for a single request.
practice_challenges.description: Implement a server-streaming RPC that sends real-time stock price updates to a client for a given ticker symbol.
practice_challenges.example_output: Client receives continuous price updates until disconnect

practice_challenges.title: Bidirectional Streaming Chat
practice_challenges.hint: Both request and response are streams. Use goroutines (Go) or asyncio (Python) to read and write concurrently.
practice_challenges.description: Implement a bidirectional streaming chat where clients send messages and receive all room messages in real-time.
practice_challenges.example_input: Client A and B connected to the same chat room
practice_challenges.example_output: Both clients see each others messages in real-time

practice_challenges.title: Deadline and Cancellation Handling
practice_challenges.hint: Set a deadline on the client side. The server should check context.is_cancelled() and clean up resources when the deadline expires.
practice_challenges.description: Implement an RPC that processes a large dataset. The client sets a 5-second deadline. If processing takes longer, the server stops and returns partial results.
practice_challenges.example_output: Returns 200K processed records + status indicating partial completion

practice_challenges.title: Interceptor for Auth and Logging
practice_challenges.hint: gRPC interceptors wrap RPC handlers. Use unary_server_interceptor for unary RPCs and stream_server_interceptor for streaming RPCs.
practice_challenges.description: Implement a server interceptor that validates JWT tokens on every RPC call, logs request duration, and returns UNAUTHENTICATED for invalid tokens.
practice_challenges.example_input: RPC call with metadata: authorization: Bearer <jwt>
practice_challenges.example_output: Valid token: request proceeds and logged. Invalid: UNAUTHENTICATED error
